title: Prefetch, Goroutine Concurrency, and Capacity · Runtime Contract: Prefetch Window Semantics
summary: A Go 1.26 service team must relate Qos prefetch, handler goroutines, bytes, downstream pools, service time, and recovery backlog; reviewers reject hidden goroutine ownership, unbounded work, stale channel state, and claims that stop at a nil client error. The learner predicts values, ownership transitions, and stop paths before seeing a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the prefetch window semantics increment in a measured consumer-capacity budget. Learners progress through prediction, ex…
objectives: Explain RabbitMQ per-consumer prefetch, deprecated global QoS, in-flight races, quorum caps, and the zero-means-unbounded hazard; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.

Use one terminal delivery decision owner to prevent timeout, abort, handler, and shutdown paths from double acknowledging or conflicting; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Predict Prefetch Window Semantics · Theory Prefetch Window Semantics
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go messaging team handles case 9850 during Go publisher supervisor losing one state notification. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine prefetch, active goroutines, dependency capacity, and message-byte budgets; retain screen-reader-readable topology and delivery evidence; then raise a broker resource alarm and halt new admission. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Explain RabbitMQ per-consumer prefetch, deprecated global QoS, in-flight races, quorum caps, and the zero-means-unbounded hazard; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
